metric_value: Most legacy mainframe apps use two-digit dates and are not Y2K safe
notes: Core premise of the study

metric_value: Applications installed recently with four-digit date codes; already compliant
notes: Least critical category

metric_value: Programs no longer needed; may account for up to 80% of application inventory
notes: Recommendation: simply discard

metric_value: E-mail, groupware, data warehousing; disruptive but not catastrophic if they fail
notes: Least likely to have Y2K problems due to relative newness

metric_value: Payroll, manufacturing, cash management — must be accurate but display errors tolerable
notes: Bulk of mainframe conversion resources focused here

metric_value: EDI links with customers/suppliers/gov agencies — incompatible date formats will break value chains
notes: Risk of losing customers if exchange files are date-incorrect

metric_value: Order-taking, invoicing, payment — must be Y2K perfect; liability litigation risk if not
notes: Highest criticality category; dereliction of duty if not corrected

metric_name: Shelfware proportion of enterprise app inventory
metric_value: Up to 80% of application inventory may be shelfware
notes: Aberdeen research finding

metric_name: Typical large enterprise electronic interchange files per week
metric_value: Over 1,000 files per week for enterprises over $200M revenue
notes: Illustrates external exchange dependency

metric_name: FASB treatment of Y2K conversion vs. replacement costs
metric_value: Conversion must be expensed; replacement can be capitalized — favors replacement on P&L
notes: FASB accounting rule creates financial incentive toward replacement

metric_name: Y2K conversion risk: operating environment certification
metric_value: Most suppliers do not know if their legacy products are Y2K safe; fearful of legal consequences
notes: Major concern flagged by Aberdeen

metric_value: 95%+ of Atlanta Olympics systems worked; journalist-facing apps failed — caused black-eye reputational damage
notes: Cited as analogy for Y2K untestability risk

metric_name: Large enterprise Y2K cost underestimation example
metric_value: One large utility initially estimated $5M; revised to $15M after expert assessment
notes: Illustrates internal vs. third-party estimation gap

metric_value: Strategic planning and supplier selection must be started by September 1997
notes: Aberdeen's recommended deadline for application acquisition decisions

metric_name: Timeline: new production systems cutover deadline
metric_value: New systems must be running parallel by September 1, 1998; production cutover by December 1, 1998
notes: 13-month operational buffer before 1/1/2000

metric_name: Timeline prediction outcome: Y2K transition disruption
metric_value: January 1, 2000 passed with minimal disruptions globally; approximately $100B spent on remediation worldwide; enterprises that modernized systems emerged more competitive
notes: Verified via Wikipedia Y2K article and US Commerce Dept. report; Aberdeen's replacement-over-conversion thesis proved correct

metric_value: European companies must support both national currencies and ECU by 1999 simultaneously with Y2K fix
notes: Cited as compounding factor for European enterprises

metric_value: Not automatically Y2K safe; many Unix OSes and PC BIOSs not originally designed for four-digit dates
notes: Counter-intuitive finding — contradicts common assumption that modern systems are safe

metric_value: Creative destruction: replace legacy apps with modern ERP/process systems wherever feasible, prioritizing customer-responsive and external-exchange applications
notes: Core thesis of the study

metric_value: All senior officers, not just IS managers, will be viewed as responsible for success or failure
notes: Governance framing; elevated Y2K to board-level issue

metric_name: ERP adoption accelerated by Y2K trigger
metric_value: Many leading manufacturers already implementing next-gen ERP, upgrading to Y2K compliance as a side effect
notes: Predicts ERP adoption wave

metric_value: Global ERP market grew from ~$5B (1996) to ~$17B (2002); SAP, Oracle, PeopleSoft all saw major growth driven by Y2K-triggered modernization; prediction confirmed
notes: Verified via industry analyst reports; ERP adoption wave materialized exactly as predicted
